globalNews.title: How elite US teams tackle combat rescue missions
globalNews.summary: Air force pararescue units who specialise in CSAR missions are among the most highly trained in the US military.

globalNews.title: Artemis II crew take 'spectacular' image of Earth
globalNews.summary: The snap was taken aboard the Orion capsule by its commander, Reid Wiseman, as the crew head towards the Moon.

globalNews.title: Burkina Faso must 'forget' about democracy, military leader says
globalNews.summary: Capt Ibrahim Traoré, who seized power in 2023, says democratic rule would not work for his country.

globalNews.title: French-owned ship passes through Strait of Hormuz
globalNews.summary: It appears to be the first ship owned by a major European firm to go through the strait since the conflict began.

globalNews.title: France's Muslim gathering ban overturned by courts
globalNews.summary: The Paris police department had argued that the four-day gathering was a security threat because it could be a target of terrorism.

cryptoNews.title: Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal
cryptoNews.summary: The non-profit foundation has staked 69,500 ETH, nearly reaching the goal it unveiled at the end of February, less than two months ago.

cryptoNews.title: Cambodian lawmakers propose severe prison time for crypto scammers
cryptoNews.summary: The draft bill, yet to be signed into law by the king, marked a significant policy change for Cambodia officials in addressing scam centers.

cryptoNews.title: Is Bitcoin mispricing a prolonged Iran war? Ex-hedge fund manager weighs in
cryptoNews.summary: In a recent Cointelegraph interview, macro investor James Lavish explains why markets are pricing in a quick end to the Iran war — and what could happen if that assumption is wrong.

cryptoNews.title: US community banks oppose OCC's approval of Coinbase trust charter
cryptoNews.summary: The Independent Community Bankers of America warns Coinbase’s trust charter falls short of regulatory standards and could pose risks to consumers and the financial system.

cryptoNews.title: Kalshi onboards ex-Democratic strategist amid legal troubles
cryptoNews.summary: Stephanie Cutter will join the prediction markets company as a policy adviser, having previously worked in Democratic lawmakers’ campaigns.

aiNews.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
aiNews.summary: In addition to Lightcap's new role, OpenAI CMO Kate Rouch will be stepping away from the company to focus on cancer recovery, with a plan to return when her health allows.

aiNews.title: Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
aiNews.summary: Anthropic has purchased the stealth biotech AI startup Coefficient Bio in a $400 million stock deal, according to The Information and Eric Newcomer.

aiNews.title: Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC
aiNews.summary: With the midterms right around the corner, the new group is positioned to back candidates who support the AI company's policy agenda.

aiNews.title: AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
aiNews.summary: Meta, Microsoft, and Google are all betting big on new natural gas power plants to run their AI data centers. They may regret it.

aiNews.title: People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center
aiNews.summary: A new poll shows that the debate over data centers is far from settled.

podcasts.title: OpenAI Acquires TBPN: (yes the tech news podcast)
Marc Andreessen on AI Winters and Agent Breakthroughs
The Chopping Block: Is Canton a Real Blockchain? Ethereum’s Cypherpunk Dilemma, AI Security Chaos
The Two Biggest Issues In Crypto Today | Weekly Roundup
ROLLUP: Google’s Quantum Warning | Trump’s Iran Speech | Ethereum Economic Zones | Drift Hack
The Bull & Bear Cases For Ethereum | Roundup
How Focus Killed Sora and Saved Anthropic | This Week in AI with Victor Riparbelli, Nick Harris & Jeremy Fraenkel
How 3 CEOs Use AI to Run $10B in Companies | This Week in AI
podcasts.summary: Three CEOs managing a combined ~$10B in company value discuss how AI is reshaping enterprise infrastructure across tabular data (Fundamental's LTM/Nexus model for fraud detection, demand forecasting, etc.), AI video for business (Synthesia, 100M+ ARR, 90% of Fortune 100), and photonic computing (Light Matter, using light instead of copper to 3x AI training speeds). The broader theme is that AI is now automating cognition itself — not just physical labor — and most people are underestimating how fast the shift is happening.
keyTakeaways: LLMs haven't solved structured/tabular data — Fundamental is building a purpose-built Large Tabular Model (LTM) with different architecture than transformers, targeting enterprise use cases like fraud detection, demand forecasting, and ETA prediction that still run on pre-LLM ML algorithms.

OpenAI shutting down Sora signals a strategic retreat to focus: the lesson is that 'doing too many things at once' kills execution, and B2B copilot/coding is the near-term value driver — Claude Code is the talk of founder circles right now.

Photonic interconnects (fiber/light vs. copper) are the new Moore's Law bottleneck — Light Matter claims 3x faster AI training times, meaning early adopters at foundation model companies will compound their advantage dramatically.

The cognitive automation moment is underappreciated: 70% of workers think AI will hurt others' jobs but only 30% worry about their own — a dangerous blind spot as white-collar/knowledge work (including accounting) is now directly in scope.

AI video for business is a proven wedge: Synthesia's bet that enterprises want video-native communication (not docs/slides) has paid off — 100M ARR with 90% Fortune 100 penetration by focusing narrowly rather than chasing Hollywood-quality generation.

podcasts.title: Do Centralized Real World Assets on DeFi Break Ethereum? - Bits + Bips
podcasts.summary: The Bits + Bips panel debates the Iran conflict's market implications, with hosts split between near-term bearishness (rising oil at $116/bbl, 10-year yields climbing, stalled negotiations) and medium-term crypto bullishness once the situation resolves. The core tension is whether a negotiated deal is even possible given Iran's fragmented leadership, hardliner incentives, and the regime's economic benefit from current elevated oil prices. Consensus is the conflict will extend well beyond the White House's initial 4-5 week timeline, keeping risk assets under pressure.
keyTakeaways: Defensively position near-term: oil above $116/bbl, 10-year yields at 4.48% (mortgage pressure), dollar strengthening, and inflation rising create a toxic backdrop for risk assets until conflict resolution.

Negotiations are structurally broken — the US doesn't have a reliable counterparty; internal IRGC fractures mean the eventual negotiating partner may not even be in power yet, pointing to a 6-12+ month timeline.
Bitcoin seen as a cyclical buying opportunity once the conflict clears, with hosts citing continued resilience and an inevitable 'catch-up to gold' narrative as a medium-term thesis.

The conflict is now a global problem, not a US one — Strait of Hormuz closure is choking Asia and threatening Europe, which increases international pressure for resolution but also raises escalation risk.
White House credibility with markets is eroding: no Pentagon briefings in 9 days, shifting timelines, and cabinet language suggesting regime change as an unstated objective are fueling uncertainty.

podcasts.title: What Happens When a Public Company Goes All In on AI
podcasts.summary: Block (parent of Square, Cash App, Afterpay) executed a 40%+ RIF in early 2026, explicitly driven by AI — not overhiring cleanup — after a step-change in agentic coding capability in late November/early December 2025 made small squads of 1-6 engineers as productive as teams of 14. Business lead Owen Jennings describes rebuilding the entire org from scratch around small squads, AI agents, and an internal agentic OS, while protecting compliance and customer trust functions. The episode is a rare inside look at what AI-native restructuring actually looks like at a public fintech company.
keyTakeaways: The productivity inflection was abrupt: a specific model capability jump in late Nov/early Dec 2025 (Opus 4.6, Codex 5.3) suddenly made AI agents effective on large, complex existing codebases — not just greenfield projects — which is what triggered the decision.

Cuts were heavily skewed toward engineering/product/design, not ops — compliance and SDR/BDR teams were barely touched, signaling the RIF was genuinely AI-productivity-driven, not financial engineering.

The new operating model is async and parallel: individuals now run 8–14 concurrent AI agents, context-switching between PRs rather than working linearly — a fundamental workflow shift for engineers, PMs, and growth marketers alike.

Block's multi-year AI infrastructure investment (Goose agent harness launched early 2024, internal agentic OS 'G2') was a prerequisite — companies without that substrate will struggle to replicate this quickly.

Jennings frames the macro as Jevons paradox: fewer engineers per product, but likely far more products and companies getting built — net engineering employment impact is uncertain, but per-product headcount is clearly shrinking.
